briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. I will now begin the climate briefing for July 24, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Global Extreme Weather and Widespread Damage
sections.content: Today's briefing begins with news of intensifying global extreme weather phenomena and their widespread damage. In Europe, 'flash droughts' are spreading water shortages and wildfire risks [10], and in Southern France, massive wildfires have led to the evacuation of thousands, with temperatures in Southern Europe reaching extreme levels, resulting in immense human and economic losses [11, 70, 71]. The UK has also suffered economic losses of 2.2 trillion KRW due to heatwaves [12], while drought has been declared in parts of Wales, and this July is expected to be the driest month in 200 years [72, 73, 74]. Climate change is increasing the likelihood of severe winter droughts in Colorado and the potential disappearance of winter in Western regions [44, 45, 48], and causing widespread ecosystem changes such as the reshaping of California's wine map and the formation of 'ghost forests' on Long Island [41, 47, 55]. Furthermore, it is severely impacting public health, including reduced nocturnal recovery time for the human body and an increased risk of anemia in women of childbearing age due to food insecurity [59, 60].

sections.title: ⚠️ Domestic and International Ecosystem Changes and Threats to the Agricultural Sector
sections.content: Domestically and internationally, the threats of climate change to ecosystems and the agricultural sector are becoming visible. With increasing greenhouse gases, a phenomenon where rice plants grow taller but grains decrease in size has been observed, raising concerns for future rice farming [4], and as repeated fish deaths due to extreme weather occur, the government has moved to legislate climate change responses for aquaculture farms [6]. The reduction in Arctic sea ice is leading to increased shipping and cloud cover, demonstrating complex changes in the climate system [7]. Notably, coral bleaching is occurring worldwide, and new studies are identifying human-induced climate change, not El Niño, as the primary cause, indicating a severe crisis for marine ecosystems [42, 49, 66].

sections.title: 🌱 Domestic and International Efforts for Climate Crisis Adaptation and Carbon Neutrality
sections.content: Amidst these crises, domestic and international efforts for climate change adaptation and carbon neutrality continue. Domestically, a climate change-adapted forage crop called 'Saryopi' has been developed, with expectations for import substitution [1], and the Rural Development Administration has been selected as a best practice for its 'National Climate Crisis Adaptation Enhancement Measures' for three consecutive years [8]. The Wonju Climate Change Promotion Hall is promoting carbon neutrality values through its special exhibition 'Net-Zero, Delivered by Moss' [2, 3, 5], and various local governments, including Gyeongsan City and Uiseong County, are promoting customized environmental education like 'Visiting Carbon Neutrality Classrooms' to encourage participation from residents and students [17, 25, 26]. Internationally, adaptation efforts continue, with CalFire testing strategies to enhance redwood forest resilience against climate change and preparing soil through microbial training [43, 52].

sections.title: 🌍 Accelerating Global Renewable Energy Transition and Strengthening Policies
sections.content: To achieve carbon neutrality goals, the acceleration of global renewable energy transition and strengthening of policies are prominent worldwide. China is taking proactive steps, legally mandating renewable energy targets for the first time and announcing its '15th Five-Year Plan for Renewable Energy Development' [27, 32], which is further deepening discussions on global RE100 and enhancing renewable energy competitiveness [28]. In the domestic aquaculture sector, the basis for new and renewable energy support has been established, and administrative procedures have been streamlined [29], while Yeonggwang County and Changwon City are actively promoting public-interest solar projects and expanding solar power generation [30, 34, 37]. Grid-forming ESS is emerging as a realistic solution for stabilizing Europe's renewable energy grid [35], and domestic renewable energy output control is confirmed to be managed stably [36], underscoring the increasing importance of renewable energy infrastructure alongside the transformation of industrial complexes in the era of carbon neutrality [24, 33].

sections.title: 📰 Social Perception and Discussion on Climate Change
sections.content: Social perception and discussion on climate change are unfolding from various perspectives. While former President Donald Trump claims climate change is a 'hoax,' Americans are not accepting his assertions [40], and debates continue, with a recently published op-ed being identified as climate change denial [51]. Meanwhile, climate change is being referred to as a 'Poly Crisis,' and the potential of humanity and the need for a paradigm shift are being discussed [53]. Amidst the growing recognition that recycling alone is not enough to stop climate change [54], the UN is set to list more heritage sites 'in danger' due to conflict or climate change [57], requiring deep reflection on the impacts of climate change across human civilization.

briefing.closing: Today's briefing demonstrates that efforts for adaptation and mitigation are simultaneously underway amidst the deepening global climate crisis. Through continuous attention and international cooperation, we must seek wise response measures to climate change. This concludes today's climate briefing.

articles.title: Thousands evacuated in south of France as wildfire rages across pine forest – video
articles.description: Local people and tourists were evacuated from the Arcachon Bay area overnight into Thursday after a huge fire engulfed more than 3,400 hectares (8,400 acres) of forest. Pushed by the wind, with flames reaching 10 metres high, the fire is still spreading towards the Cap Ferret peninsula. On Tuesday, two firefighters died battling another nearby fire close to Bordeaux airport Thousands flee wildfires in France as temperatures reach extreme levels in southern Europe Continue reading...

summary.phenomenon: Thousands of local residents and tourists were evacuated from France's Arcachon Bay area overnight into Thursday after a massive wildfire engulfed over 3,400 hectares (8,400 acres) of forest. On Tuesday, two firefighters died while battling another wildfire near Bordeaux airport.
summary.cause: Amid extreme temperatures observed in Southern Europe, strong winds propelled the flames up to 10 meters high, contributing to the wildfire's spread.
summary.outlook: The wildfire continues to spread towards the Cap Ferret peninsula.

articles.original_description: Local people and tourists were evacuated from the Arcachon Bay area overnight into Thursday after a huge fire engulfed more than 3,400 hectares (8,400 acres) of forest. On Tuesday, two firefighters died battling another nearby fire close to Bordeaux airport Thousands flee wildfires in France as temperatures reach extreme levels in southern Europe Continue reading...

articles.original_title: Thousands flee wildfires in France as temperatures reach extreme levels in southern Europe
articles.description: Some parts of Mediterranean Sea 5C hotter than normal, with air temperatures above 44C expected in SpainBrutal heat torching southern Europe has forced nearly 20,000 people to flee wildfires in France as a marine heatwave scalds the Mediterranean and researchers warn that “Dantesque” scenarios cannot be ruled out as infernos worsen.A “particularly intense” wildfire on France’s southwestern Atlantic coast prompted the evacuation of more than 18,800 people, including thousands of tourists from cam

summary.phenomenon: Extreme heat has occurred in parts of Southern Europe, with temperatures exceeding 44C, and some areas of the Mediterranean Sea becoming 5C hotter than normal. This has led to the evacuation of over 18,800 people due to wildfires on France's southwestern Atlantic coast.
summary.cause: A marine heatwave affecting the Mediterranean Sea has led to rising temperatures and exacerbated wildfires.
summary.outlook: Researchers have warned that 'Dantesque' scenarios cannot be ruled out as wildfires worsen.

articles.original_title: Drought declared in parts of Wales as July on course to be driest in nearly 200 years
articles.description: Government agency announces status in north Wales and Upper Severn after series of heatwaves and lack of rainfallParts of Wales are now in drought and all other areas of the country are experiencing prolonged dry conditions, with this month on course to be the driest on record for nearly 200 years.Natural Resources Wales (NRW) said on Thursday that record-low river flows, back-to-back heatwaves and widespread wildfires have prompted the declaration of drought status in north Wales and the Upper

summary.phenomenon: Drought has been declared in North Wales and the Upper Severn region, with the entire country of Wales experiencing prolonged dry conditions. This July is projected to be the driest on record in nearly 200 years.
summary.cause: A series of heatwaves, lack of rainfall, record-low river flows, consecutive heatwaves, and widespread wildfires are the causes for the drought declaration.

articles.title: The Guardian view on Britain’s uninhabitable homes: as temperatures rise, a new approach is needed | Editorial
articles.description: Sadiq Khan’s heat-ready plan for London is a sensible strategy for adaptation that should provide a roadmap for other cities“Our ability to trust that our homes will keep us safe from the outside is under threat,” the geographer Sam Johnson-Schlee writes in Hot House, his recent book on how heat has transformed domestic life. This summer, high temperatures have become inescapable. People have coped with makeshift solutions: keeping curtains drawn, placing foil over windows or – for those who can

summary.phenomenon: High temperatures have become an inescapable reality in the UK this summer, with people resorting to makeshift solutions such as drawing curtains or placing foil on windows.
summary.outlook: London Mayor Sadiq Khan's 'heat-ready plan' is regarded as a sensible adaptation strategy that could provide a roadmap for other cities.

articles.original_title: I am used to hot days now. But the warm wind has really panicked me | Adrian Chiles
articles.description: I first noticed it a couple of weeks ago on the streets of Cardiff – and it felt alarmingly wrongWhen the wind is warm, you know something is up. Naturally, I have been well aware of the awful reality of the climate crisis, but it has taken the warmth of the wind to really panic me. Not the endless 30C+ days, the desiccated garden, the tap slowing to a drip. No, it is the warm wind blowing on me. I can just about manage in punishing heat, even though it is particularly suffocating when the air i

summary.phenomenon: The author first noticed warm winds blowing on the streets of Cardiff a few weeks ago, which occurred alongside endless days exceeding 30C, desiccated gardens, and taps slowing to a drip.
